title: Cosine bands, flat bands and superconductivity in orthorhombic iron selenide
authors: Ian D R Mackinnon, Jose A Alarco
abstract: Electronic band structures (EBSs) for orthorhombic beta FeSe1-x at less than 16 K and up to 23 GPa using experimentally determined cell dimensions are evaluated for cosine-shaped bands near, or crossing, EF. Cosine shaped bands are present in reciprocal directions parallel to the c axis at all pressures. Calculations using a P1 cell derived from Cmma symmetry with a 2c superlattice moderates the effect of intersecting bands to 9.0 GPa. This approach enables determination of a superconducting gap consistent with experimentally determined values. Key influences on charge distribution and transfer in the interplanar region of beta FeSe1-x are lone pair electrons which feature as flat bands (FBs) near EF along GZ in an EBS. FBs also influence the topology of Fermi surfaces as pressure increases and in directions parallel to the c* direction (i.e. offset along ky) within the Brillouin zone. At the Fermi surface along b*, cosine bands split and align favorably for electron-hole pairing with nodal inflection points located at EF. For P greater than 12.0 GPa, FBs interact with folded cosine bands invoking additional band dispersions. These calculations suggest that FBs participate in, and with increased pressure, enhance and sustain the superconducting properties of beta FeSe1-x to 23 GPa.
summary: This paper explores the relationship between electronic band topology and superconductivity in iron selenide under high pressure. It identifies that the interaction between flat bands (originating from Selenium lone pairs) and cosine-shaped bands is a key driver for the enhancement of the superconducting transition temperature. The researchers demonstrate that the peak in Tc at 9 GPa corresponds to specific band energy convergences. The study provides a microscopic view of how pressure-induced structural changes and charge transfer mechanisms sustain superconductivity up to 23 GPa.
main_problem: Investigating how pressure-induced changes in the electronic band structure, specifically the interaction between flat bands and cosine-shaped bands, influence superconductivity in orthorhombic beta-FeSe1-x.
main_result: The study finds that the energy convergence of flat bands and cosine bands at approximately 9.0 GPa coincides with the experimental peak in Tc, suggesting that flat bands help enhance and sustain superconductivity up to 23 GPa.
method: Ab initio Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using LDA and GGA functionals, combined with a P1 2c superlattice approach and COOP analysis.
model_or_system: The physical system is orthorhombic beta-FeSe1-x (specifically x=0.01 and x=0.03) under high pressure (up to 23 GPa), focusing on the role of Selenium lone pair electrons and their impact on band topology.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), superconducting gap, electronic band structures (EBS), Fermi surface topology, and electron density difference (EDD).
important_parameters: Pressure regimes from 0 to 23 GPa; temperature regimes from 5 K to 16 K; energy scales in meV.
main_assumptions: Use of a P1 superlattice to moderate band intersections; reliance on experimentally determined cell dimensions for DFT input.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Tc vs pressure and diffraction data; Figure 2 shows crystal structure schematics; Figure 3 displays calculated band structures and FB energy shifts; Figure 4 tracks energy changes; Figure 5 compares calculated and experimental Tc; Figure 6 illustrates band intersections and Fermi surface orientations; Figure 7 shows electron density difference plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of pressure-induced superconductivity, describes the structural and computational models (DFT/superlattice), analyzes the evolution of band structures and Fermi surfaces under pressure, discusses the role of Selenium lone pairs in bonding and charge transfer, and concludes by correlating band topology changes with experimental Tc trends.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
